summary:
The spread of Hindu kingdoms across Southeast Asia (c. 1st–10th centuries AD) was one of the most remarkable processes of cultural diffusion in world history — the voluntary adoption of Indian religious, political, and artistic models by indigenous Southeast Asian societies across a vast arc from Java and Sumatra to the mainland of Indochina, creating what scholars have called the 'Indianized' world of Southeast Asia. This process occurred without military conquest: Indian traders, priests, and scholars arrived by sea, and local elites found Indian models of divine kingship, Sanskrit literacy, Hindu and Buddhist cosmology, and Indian artistic conventions extraordinarily useful for organizing and legitimizing their increasingly complex states.

The results were architecturally spectacular: the temples of Borobudur and Prambanan in Java, the Hindu-Buddhist towers of [[event:2093|Champa]], the Cham and Khmer temple complexes, and ultimately [[event:2069|Angkor Wat]] (the largest religious building ever constructed) all reflect this Indianization process. Each region adapted Indian models distinctively — the resulting civilizations were not simply copies of India but creative syntheses that blended Indian frameworks with local traditions, languages, and aesthetics. Sanskrit inscriptions appear from Java to southern Vietnam to Cambodia from the 4th century onward, documenting the spread of Indian writing systems and court culture. The Hindu kingdoms of Southeast Asia created one of the world's great civilizational zones, whose artistic heritage remains visible in the cultural landscapes of modern Thailand, Cambodia, Indonesia, and Vietnam.
